𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Nine -- Complete

Some Works of the Torah

MIQSAT MA'ASE HA-TORAH -- 4QMMT

A Legal Letter From the Qumran Community -- DSS Attested

Sections A Through C

These Are Some of the Works of the Torah -- The 364-Day Calendar -- Selected Legal Rulings on Purity -- We Have Separated From the Majority of the People -- Think of the Kings of Yisra'EL -- It Shall Be Reckoned to You as Righteousness

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 4 Qumran -- 4Q394-399 -- Hebrew Primary

Possibly Written by the Teacher of Righteousness to the High Priest

The Founding Document of the Qumran Separation

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

MIQSAT MAASE HA-TORAH -- 4QMMT -- THE FOUNDING LETTER

4QMMT — SECTION A: THE CALENDAR These Are Some of the Works of the Torah — The Calendar of 364 Days — The Appointed Times of YHWH — The Months and the Festivals — Written in the Book of Mosheh

1 These are some of the works of the Torah which we have determined, all of them concerning the appointed times and concerning Yisra'EL and their purity.

2 Concerning the calendar of years and months and appointed festivals: we hold that you should observe the calendar of the seasons as it is written.

3 The year is three hundred and sixty-four days, and the seasons are written in the book of the divisions of times as Mosheh commanded.

4 On the first of the month, on the third of the week, is the feast of the first barley — the waving of the omer.

5 After it, on the first day of the week, is the feast of weeks — fifty days from the waving of the omer.

6 On the first of the seventh month is the feast of trumpets — the day of memorial.

7 On the tenth of the seventh month is the Day of Atonement — the holiest of all the appointed times.

8 On the fifteenth of the seventh month begins the feast of booths, seven days.

9 On the twenty-second of the seventh month is the day of assembly — the eighth day.

10 On the first of the eighth month is the feast of oil — the feast of the anointing.

11 On the first of the eleventh month is the feast of wood — for the offering of the wood for the altar.

12 These are the appointed times of YHWH which you shall proclaim as holy convocations at their appointed times.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QMMT (Miqsat Ma'ase HaTorah — Some Works of the Torah) was found in Cave 4 at Qumran in multiple copies — 4Q394-399. It is one of the most important Qumran documents for understanding the community's origins and theology. The document is a legal letter — addressed by the community's leader, most likely the Teacher of Righteousness, to the Jerusalem establishment, most likely the high priest. Section A concerns the covenant calendar — the 364-day solar calendar of the Yovelim tradition — and constitutes the community's foundational challenge to the Jerusalem Temple's calendar practices.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canon preserves the book of Yovelim in full — the primary calendar authority cited in 4QMMT Section A's reference to the book of the divisions of times. The calendar of 364 days is the Yovelim calendar — 52 weeks exactly, with the major festivals falling on the same day of the week every year without variation. The Qumran community's insistence on this calendar as the only legitimate covenant calendar is the direct expression of the Yovelim tradition that the Orit Ge'ez preserves as canonical.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 23 — the chapter of appointed times — provides the foundational covenant framework for the festivals listed in 4QMMT Section A. The Qumran community's calendar interpretation differs from the later Masoretic practice in placing the waving of the omer and the feast of weeks on fixed days of the week — always Sunday — rather than variable dates. This difference in calendar interpretation was the primary cause of the community's separation from the Jerusalem Temple establishment.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The calendar dispute underlying 4QMMT Section A is not a minor liturgical disagreement. For the Qumran community, observing the correct covenant calendar was a matter of fundamental covenant faithfulness: celebrating YHWH's appointed times on the wrong days — as the Jerusalem Temple did — was a covenant violation of the highest order. The Community Rule Column 1 states that the men of the community will not depart from any of Creator's commands concerning their appointed times. The calendar is the covenant structure of time itself.

4QMMT — SECTION B: SELECTED LEGAL RULINGS These Are the Works We Have Separated From — Concerning the Purity of the Sanctuary — Concerning the Sacrifices — Concerning the Priests — We Have Separated From the Majority of the People

1 Concerning the offerings: we hold that one must not offer on the altar grain that has been mixed or soaked together — everything offered must be pure.

2 Concerning the hides of the cattle and of the flocks: we hold that hides shall not be brought into the holy city from any animal slaughtered outside the camp.

3 For you know that the place where the Presence rests is more holy than any other place — therefore its requirements of purity are greater.

4 Concerning the offering of the Gentiles: we hold that their offerings are unclean and their sacrifices are not to be received in the sanctuary.

5 Concerning the priests who serve before YHWH: no priest who has an impurity shall serve at the altar or enter the inner court.

6 Concerning the blind and the deaf: they shall not serve in the sanctuary because those who serve must see and hear the required procedures.

7 Concerning the purity of the city of the sanctuary: all those who have any form of corpse impurity shall not enter it.

8 Concerning the liquid streams: we hold that streams of liquid poured from one vessel to another carry impurity from the lower vessel to the upper.

9 Concerning the dogs: they shall not be brought into the holy city because they eat the bones of the sanctuary dead and the holy flesh.

10 Concerning the fruit of Yisra'EL: the first fruits of grain, wine, and oil are sacred to the priests and shall not be eaten by those in a state of impurity.

11 These are the works we have separated from and we have separated ourselves from the majority of the people because of these matters.

12 And we have not mixed with them in these things nor in these matters — for you know that no rebellion or deceit is in our hands.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QMMT Section B is the legal core of the document — a list of specific halakhic rulings on which the Qumran community disagreed with the Jerusalem establishment. The rulings cover temple purity, sacrificial requirements, priestly fitness, and the purity of the holy city. Each ruling begins with the phrase we hold — anachnu omrim — establishing that these are not individual opinions but the community's authoritative legal positions. Section B is the clearest surviving evidence of the specific legal disputes that caused the community to separate from the Jerusalem Temple.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves the covenant purity laws of Vayikra and Bamidbar that underlie 4QMMT Section B's legal rulings. The Qumran community's stricter interpretations — particularly concerning liquid purity streams, verse 8 — go beyond what the Masoretic Vayikra text explicitly states, reflecting the community's conviction that their covenant interpretation represents the full intent of the Mosaic Torah even where the explicit text is general. The Orit Ge'ez and the Qumran legal tradition both take the covenant purity laws with absolute seriousness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 11-15 — the purity legislation — and Bamidbar 19 — corpse impurity — provide the foundational covenant texts for Section B's rulings. The Qumran community's legal positions in 4QMMT represent stricter interpretations of the same Mosaic covenant law that the later rabbinic tradition interpreted differently. The separation between the Qumran community and the Jerusalem establishment is fundamentally a dispute about who has the correct interpretation of the covenant Torah — both sides claimed the authority of Mosheh.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — these are the works we have separated from and we have separated ourselves from the majority of the people because of these matters. This is the most explicit statement of the community's covenant separation in any Qumran document. The phrase some works of the Torah — miqsat ma'ase ha-torah — in the document's title refers to these specific legal rulings. The community has not left the covenant people; it has separated from the majority's incorrect practice of the covenant. The separation is a covenant act, not a rejection of the covenant.

4QMMT — SECTION C: THE EXHORTATION Think of the Kings of Yisra'EL and Their Works — Consider the End of Each of Them — David Was a Man of Covenant Faithfulness — It Shall Be Reckoned to You as Righteousness — If You Do What Is Right

1 Now we have written to you some of the works of the Torah which we hold are good for you and for your people — for we see that you have wisdom and knowledge of the Torah.

2 Consider all these things and ask him to strengthen your will and to remove from you the plans of evil and the devices of Belial.

3 That you may rejoice at the end of time when you find that some of our words are true.

4 And it shall be reckoned to you as righteousness when you do what is upright and good before him for your own good and for the good of Yisra'EL.

5 Think of the kings of Yisra'EL and consider their works — each of them who feared the Torah was delivered from afflictions.

6 And the seekers of the Torah were forgiven their transgressions.

7 Think of Dawid — he was a man of covenant faithfulness and he was also delivered from many afflictions and forgiven.

8 We have also written to you about some of the works of the Torah — those which we think are good for you and for your people.

9 For the foundation of your wisdom is that you seek understanding and knowledge of the Torah — and consider and reflect on the end.

10 And remember that the kings of Yisra'EL and the princes of Yehudah — all of them — were brought low because of their abandonment of the Torah.

11 For the curse has been sent upon us — the curse that was written in the Torah of Mosheh — because we have sinned against him.

12 Remember the former kings and see that each of those who honored the Torah was rescued and delivered from all his afflictions.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QMMT Section C is the exhortatory conclusion of the legal letter. The document shifts from legal ruling to pastoral appeal — the author urges the recipient to consider the legal positions outlined and to act on them for his own good and the good of Yisra'EL. The appeal to the kings of Yisra'EL — particularly Dawid as a man of covenant faithfulness who was forgiven despite his sins — is the document's most personal and pastoral moment. The phrase it shall be reckoned to you as righteousness — in reference to doing what is right before Creator — echoes the Bereshit 15:6 language of Avraham's faith.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves the covenant history of the kings of Yisra'EL in Melakhim Aleph and Bet and Divrei HaYamim — the same history that Section C appeals to. The covenant pattern of the kings — those who feared the Torah were delivered, those who abandoned it were brought low — is the consistent theological interpretation of the covenant history that both the Orit Ge'ez and the Qumran tradition maintain. 4QMMT Section C applies this covenant historical pattern as a direct appeal to the recipient: choose the path of the kings who honored the Torah.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 28-30 — the covenant blessings and curses and the appeal to return — provides the foundational covenant framework for Section C's exhortation. The phrase the curse that was written in the Torah of Mosheh — verse 11 — is a direct citation of Devarim 29:20. The letter ends by placing the covenant community's situation within the covenant curse of Devarim: the exile and affliction they experience is the covenant curse of Devarim 28 operating — and the return to the Torah is the covenant path of Devarim 30.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The phrase it shall be reckoned to you as righteousness — verse 4 — in the context of performing specific legal works has been noted by scholars as the earliest example of the connection between specific covenant actions and the reckoning of righteousness. This is not a claim that legal performance earns righteousness — the document is clear that righteousness is Creator's gift. Rather it reflects the covenant conviction that genuine covenant faithfulness — including specific legal observance — is the context in which Creator's righteousness is extended to his people. The covenant is lived out in specific practices, not merely affirmed in general commitments.

4QMMT -- SOME WORKS OF THE TORAH -- COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Nine -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

IT SHALL BE RECKONED TO YOU AS RIGHTEOUSNESS

WHEN YOU DO WHAT IS UPRIGHT AND GOOD BEFORE HIM

4QMMT Section C -- Dead Sea Scrolls 4Q394-399
